card.intuition: Store partial sums indexed by the lowest set bit of the index — point updates and prefix sum queries each take exactly O(log n) steps by flipping or clearing that lowest bit.
card.analogy: A warehouse with bins sized 1, 2, 4, 8… Each bin covers a range equal to its size. Adding stock to a position updates only the bins that 'contain' it (by bit manipulation). Querying a prefix sums only the bins needed to cover it.
card.time_complexity: O(n) build; O(log n) point update; O(log n) prefix sum query
card.space_complexity: O(n) — simpler and lighter than a segment tree
when_to_use: Point update + prefix sum queries — the classic Fenwick use case
Counting inversions in O(n log n) — process elements right-to-left, query how many smaller elements already seen
Offline range sum queries after coordinate compression
When you need a simpler alternative to a segment tree for sum queries only
gotchas: Fenwick trees are 1-indexed — always shift array indices by +1; tree[0] is unused
Update traversal: i += i & (-i) to move to the next responsible index (add lowest set bit)
Query traversal: i -= i & (-i) to peel off the lowest set bit and walk toward index 1

exercise.prompt: Complete the Fenwick tree update and prefix-sum query using the lowest-set-bit trick.
hints: Update: i += i & (-i) adds the lowest set bit to move to the next index that 'covers' position i
Query: i -= i & (-i) removes the lowest set bit to sum decreasing ranges toward index 0
exercise.test_code:
ft = FenwickTree(6)
for idx, val in enumerate([1,3,5,7,9,11], start=1):
    ft.update(idx, val)
assert ft.prefix_sum(3) == 9,   'prefix(3): 1+3+5'
assert ft.prefix_sum(6) == 36,  'prefix(6): all'
assert ft.range_sum(2,4) == 15, 'range(2,4): 3+5+7'
assert ft.range_sum(4,4) == 7,  'single element'
ft.update(2, 7)   # 3 -> 10
assert ft.prefix_sum(3) == 16,  'after update: 1+10+5'
assert ft.range_sum(1,6) == 43, 'full sum after update'
print('PASS')
